evidence_citations.excerpt: I keep staring at the empty canvas in my studio, wondering if I’m doing this right without her to critique me.
I keep thinking the memorial site has to be perfect, and the idea that I might mess it up feels like I'm failing her

transcript.text: I keep staring at the empty canvas in my studio, wondering if I’m doing this right without her to critique me. Every night I replay that last drive we took together, and the nightmares make it hard to sleep. I know the memorial site is overdue, but it feels like I'm just dragging my feet on a project that never mattered until she was there.

You're sitting in your studio, staring at that empty canvas, and it's like the silence is deafening without your mom's critique. The memories of that last drive together are still haunting you, and it's no wonder you're struggling to sleep. It takes a lot of courage to admit you're feeling stuck, especially when it comes to something that mattered so much to your mom. Can you tell me more about what's been going through your mind when you think about the memorial site project, and how you've been trying to navigate your feelings about it?

I don't know if I can even get past this. Every time I think about launching the site, I get this wave of guilt that I'm not honoring her enough, and then I just shut it down… It's like my career is stuck, and I can't sleep because the nightmares keep replaying our last drive.

You're feeling overwhelmed by guilt every time you think about launching the memorial site, and it's like that's freezing you in your career. It's no wonder you can't sleep with those nightmares about your last drive together still haunting you. What's the hardest part for you about moving forward with the site - is it the fear of not honoring your mom's memory, or something else entirely?

I keep thinking the memorial site has to be perfect, and the idea that I might mess it up feels like I'm failing her. That guilt keeps me from starting, and every time I try I end up scrolling through old photos instead. Sleep is even worse—nightmares keep replaying our last drive. I just don't know if I can move forward or if this is just a sign that my career is stuck.

The fear of messing up the memorial site is really holding you back, and it's like that guilt is a constant reminder that you're not doing enough to honor your mom's memory. It's no wonder you're feeling stuck in your career, given the weight of that responsibility. You've been trying to navigate this emotional landscape, but it's clear that the pressure to make everything perfect is overwhelming. Can you think of a small step you could take to start moving forward with the memorial site, even if it doesn't feel perfect?

I tried to follow your idea but ended up scrolling through her photo album again. I can't even think about a blank page because every image feels like a reminder of what I could miss. How do I even start if I keep getting pulled back into the past?

You were trying to make progress on the memorial site, but the photo album pulled you back in. It's like every image is a reminder of what you could miss, and that's making it hard for you to think about a blank page. Your mind keeps wandering back to memories of your mom, and it's no wonder you're feeling stuck. What if you allowed yourself to acknowledge those feelings and memories, and then took a small step towards the memorial site - maybe just setting up the page or writing a single sentence?

I keep scrolling through her photos instead of setting up the page, and every time I try to even write a sentence I feel like I'm cutting something off. The nightmares keep looping that last drive and I just can't sleep through the day. It feels like my whole career is on hold because I can't even start this project without feeling like I'm failing her.

You're feeling like you're cutting something off every time you try to write a sentence, and that's a really painful feeling. It's like you're being pulled back into the past by your mom's photos, and the nightmares from your last drive together are still haunting you. I can imagine how exhausting it must be to feel like you're failing her, and how that's holding you back from starting the memorial site project. It takes a lot of courage to admit you're feeling stuck, especially when it comes to something that mattered so much to your mom. Yeah, it's okay to feel overwhelmed - it's a lot to carry. What's the smallest thing you could do to start moving forward, even if it doesn't feel like enough?

I don't even know what a small step would be; every time I try to open a new document I feel guilty and get dragged back to her. I keep scrolling through her photos instead of starting the site, and the nightmares keep me up at night. I feel like my career is on hold because I can't move forward.

You're feeling really guilty whenever you try to start something new, like the memorial site, and it's pulling you back to your mom. It's like every photo is a reminder of what you could miss, and that's making it hard for you to think about moving forward. Yeah, it's okay to feel overwhelmed - it's a lot to carry. What's one thing that you think might help you feel more connected to your mom's memory, even if it's not part of the memorial site project?

I keep scrolling through her photos instead of starting the site, and the nightmares keep me up at night. Maybe I should write a letter to her, but I can't even get past the first sentence. I feel like my career is on hold because I can't move forward.

You're feeling stuck and guilty whenever you try to start the memorial site, and it's like every photo of your mom is pulling you back into the past. That must be really painful for you. I can see how the pressure to honor her memory is weighing on you, and it's understandable that you're struggling to move forward. Maybe writing a letter to her could be a way to process some of these emotions and help you feel more connected to her memory. What do you think is the hardest part about getting started with that letter?

Every time I try to write a line, it feels like I'm saying goodbye too soon, and that guilt just pulls me back into the nightmare loop. I can't even sleep properly because the drive still plays over and over. My design work feels on hold because I keep waiting for the right words that never come.

The guilt you're feeling every time you try to write a line is really powerful, and it's like it's pulling you back into the past. You're trying to find a way to honor your mom's memory, but at the same time, you're feeling overwhelmed by the pressure to make everything perfect. It's like you're being pulled in two different directions, and that's making it hard for you to move forward. Can you tell me more about what's going on when you try to write, and what's holding you back from taking that first step?

I keep thinking that every word I write is a betrayal, like I'm cutting her off before she even gets to say goodbye. The nightmares keep looping the drive, and I can't sleep, so I'm stuck in a loop of guilt and paralysis. My work feels on hold because I can't even start the site or the letter. I don't know if I can ever get past this.

You're feeling like every word you write is a betrayal, and that's a really heavy burden to carry. It's like you're being pulled back into the past, and the guilt is making it hard for you to move forward. I can see how the pressure to honor your mom's memory is weighing on you, and it's understandable that you're struggling to start the memorial site or even write a letter to her. Yeah, it's okay to feel overwhelmed - it's a lot to carry. What's one thing that you think might help you feel more connected to your mom's memory, even if it's not part of the memorial site project?

I think if I just sketch something that reminds me of her—like the road we took—and jot a note in a journal, it might feel less like a big memorial and more like me carrying her memory in my everyday work. That could help me start moving, even if it’s tiny.

You're considering sketching something that reminds you of your mom, like the road you took, and jotting a note in a journal. That could be a powerful way to carry her memory with you in your everyday work, and it might feel less overwhelming than trying to create a big memorial. What do you think it is about sketching and journaling that might help you feel more connected to your mom's memory, and how could you incorporate that into your daily life?

response.final_response: You're feeling like every word you write is a betrayal, and that's a really heavy burden to carry. It's like you're being pulled back into the past, and the guilt is making it hard for you to move forward. I can see how the pressure to honor your mom's memory is weighing on you, and it's understandable that you're struggling to start the memorial site or even write a letter to her. Yeah, it's okay to feel overwhelmed - it's a lot to carry. What's one thing that you think might help you feel more connected to your mom's memory, even if it's not part of the memorial site project?

turn_traces.user_message: I think if I just sketch something that reminds me of her—like the road we took—and jot a note in a journal, it might feel less like a big memorial and more like me carrying her memory in my everyday work. That could help me start moving, even if it’s tiny.

candidate_bundle.intent_entry_style: Reflect both sides of the ambivalence before any nudge.

response.reasoning: I'm here to support this person as they navigate their ambivalence about honoring their mom's memory while being true to themselves, and they need help reflecting both sides of this decision. The strategies that might be helpful in this situation are advise_with_permission, closed_question, and structure, as they are all relevant to the preparation stage. The evidence that stands out to me is the user's desire to sketch something that reminds them of their mom and jot a note in a journal, which could be a way to carry her memory in their everyday work. This approach might help them start moving forward, even if it's a small step. My entry point will be to acknowledge the user's desire to find a way to honor their mom's memory that feels authentic to them.
